title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a professional perfumer called Abbi Ward talking about her training and the creation of a new fragrance. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a professional perfumer called Abbi Ward talking about her training and the creation of a new fragrance. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Abbi Ward explains the sensory and scientific process behind luxury perfume design.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Abbi. In the industry, we are often called 'Noses', which is a bit of a strange title, but it accurately describes our primary tool. My journey into the world of scent didn't start in a luxury boutique; it actually began when I worked at a [7]botanical garden[/7] during my university holidays. I became obsessed with how the scent of a flower could change depending on the time of day or the humidity in the air. That fascination led me to Grasse, in France, the spiritual home of perfumery.

Training to be a perfumer is like learning a new language. You have to memorize thousands of individual raw materials. During my apprenticeship, the most difficult thing to master was the [8]scent memory[/8] required to identify synthetic molecules alongside natural oils. We were tested every morning; we had to name twenty different substances blindly. If you can't recall the exact profile of a scent, you can't begin to blend it.

When I start creating a new fragrance, I don't just start mixing liquids. I start with a 'brief'—a story or a feeling that the client wants to evoke. For my latest project, the inspiration was a walk through a pine forest after rain. I found that using [9]oak moss[/9] provided the perfect earthy base note to ground the lighter, fresher elements. It’s all about balance; a single drop of the wrong ingredient can overpower the entire composition.

Chemistry is just as important as creativity. We have to consider how different molecules interact over time. I’ve noticed that the [10]evaporation rate[/10] of citrus notes is much faster than that of woody notes, which is why the 'top notes' of a perfume disappear so quickly. We use 'fixatives' to slow this process down, ensuring the scent lingers on the skin for more than just a few minutes.

Sustainability has become a major focus for us recently. Many traditional ingredients, like certain types of sandalwood, are now endangered. I was thrilled to find a supplier using [11]recycled sawdust[/11] from the furniture industry to create a high-quality aromatic oil. It’s a wonderful way to reduce our environmental impact while still producing a luxury product. Innovation in 'green chemistry' is truly changing the way we work.

People often ask how to choose the right perfume. I always tell them that [12]skin chemistry[/12] is the most important factor. A fragrance that smells incredible on a piece of card might smell completely different on you because of your natural oils and pH levels. You should always wear a scent for at least four hours before deciding to buy it.

As the digital world expands, we are even seeing attempts at 'digital scent'. However, I am skeptical. I believe the [13]emotional connection[/13] we have with physical smells is too complex to be replicated by a machine. A certain scent can trigger a childhood memory in a split second, and that is a biological miracle that belongs to the real world.

Finally, I’m currently collaborating with a museum to recreate the [14]historical scents[/14] of the 18th century for a new exhibition. It’s a fascinating piece of detective work, looking through old journals to find forgotten recipes. I hope it will give visitors a new way to experience history. Thank you for listening.
